letter_metadata.one_sentence_synthesis: The 2005 framework emphasizes intrinsic-value estimation with humility, per-share capital allocation discipline, insurance risk economics, and the primacy of resilience over accounting optics or leverage-enhanced growth.

framework_items.section: Core meta-principle: estimate intrinsic value, but stay humble about precision
framework_items.principle_statement: Intrinsic value is the key anchor for analysis, but precision varies with the predictability and simplicity of the business.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Intrinsic value is the anchor of analysis, but confidence in the estimate depends on business predictability, balance-sheet strength, and analytical simplicity.
metric_definition: intrinsic_value: estimated economic worth of the business based on future cash generation
earnings_stability: consistency and predictability of the earnings stream over time
liquidity: availability of cash or near-cash resources to meet obligations and support resilience
debt_level: amount of leverage that can narrow or widen the range of likely outcomes
metric_usage_context: intrinsic_value: serves as the central valuation anchor when comparing price with business value
earnings_stability: helps determine how narrow or wide the valuation error band should be
liquidity: supports confidence that adverse scenarios will not force distress
debt_level: helps judge whether future economics are analyzable or fragile
metric_limitations: intrinsic_value: it is never perfectly knowable and becomes less precise when future economics are uncertain
earnings_stability: past stability may overstate future predictability in complex businesses
liquidity: strong liquidity does not by itself make a difficult business easy to value
debt_level: low debt alone does not remove complexity risk
performance_logic: More predictable cash generation, stronger liquidity, and lower leverage reduce valuation uncertainty and make intrinsic value estimates more reliable.
evaluation_questions: How predictable are future cash flows?
How wide is the valuation error range?
What makes intrinsic value easier or harder to estimate here?
Can the business be understood at the unit level?
diagnostic_signals: Complex operations that widen the error band
practical_rules: Use wider valuation bands when economics are hard to forecast.
Prefer businesses whose cash generation can be understood with confidence.
example_buffett_phrasing: the wider the error band in valuation

framework_items.section: Analyze by economic components, not only by consolidated accounting
framework_items.principle_statement: When a company contains many different businesses, consolidated statements are insufficient for real understanding.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Analyze diversified companies by the economics of their major components rather than relying only on consolidated accounting.
metric_definition: segment_earnings: earnings power of each major operating or investment component
sum_of_the_parts_value: estimated value of the whole derived from valuing major pieces separately
metric_usage_context: segment_earnings: used to understand what each business contributes on a stand-alone basis
sum_of_the_parts_value: used to test whether the parent structure adds or subtracts value relative to its parts
metric_limitations: segment_earnings: consolidated reporting can hide weak and strong units inside one total
sum_of_the_parts_value: a parts-based estimate can still mislead if segment economics are poorly understood
performance_logic: Different business units have different economics and capital needs; separating them improves valuation and capital-allocation judgment.
evaluation_questions: What are the major value components?
What are the stand-alone economics of each component?
Does the parent allocate capital better than subsidiaries could on their own?
diagnostic_signals: Large gap between consolidated totals and segment economics
Parent complexity with unclear value creation
practical_rules: Break diversified businesses into logical segments before valuing them.
Ask whether the holding-company structure creates value.

framework_items.section: Use dual lenses of value: investments per share and operating earnings per share
framework_items.principle_statement: For conglomerates or hybrid businesses, value can come from investment assets and from operating earnings power.
framework_items.normalized_principle: In hybrid businesses, track both asset value per share and operating earnings per share to understand how value is compounding.
metric_definition: investments_per_share: per-share value of marketable assets and investments owned
operating_earnings_per_share: per-share earnings power generated by operating businesses
per_share_value: value attributable to each share rather than total corporate size
metric_usage_context: investments_per_share: used to track value created by capital allocation and appreciating assets
operating_earnings_per_share: used to judge the growth of operating subsidiaries
per_share_value: used to ensure growth benefits owners on a per-share basis
metric_limitations: investments_per_share: asset values can fluctuate with markets and may not represent permanent earning power
operating_earnings_per_share: can be distorted by abnormal years or accounting items
per_share_value: total growth can mislead if share count rises materially
performance_logic: A company may create value through appreciating investments, operating businesses, or both; per-share tracking shows whether owners actually benefit.
evaluation_questions: What portion of value comes from investment assets?
What portion comes from operating earnings power?
Is per-share value compounding or only absolute size?
diagnostic_signals: Rising total value with weak per-share progress
One engine growing at the expense of the other
practical_rules: Track both asset-based and operating-based value drivers.
Judge progress per share, not in total dollars.

framework_items.section: Distrust flattering growth rates unless the base year is economically normal
framework_items.principle_statement: Growth statistics can mislead when the starting or ending year is aberrational.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Evaluate growth only after checking whether the base period is economically normal.
metric_definition: compound_annual_growth_rate: annualized growth rate over a multiyear period
earnings_growth: change in earnings over time from one period to another
metric_usage_context: compound_annual_growth_rate: used to summarize growth across a period after checking whether endpoints are representative
earnings_growth: used to distinguish true progress from arithmetic rebound
metric_limitations: compound_annual_growth_rate: can look impressive when the starting year was depressed
earnings_growth: a rebound from abnormal weakness may not indicate durable improvement
performance_logic: Reported growth is only meaningful when it reflects normalized economics rather than a bounce from unusually weak starting conditions.
evaluation_questions: Was the base year normal?
Is reported growth mostly arithmetic rebound?
What does growth look like over a full cycle?
diagnostic_signals: Very high growth after a depressed starting year
Weak unit economics beneath strong headline CAGR
practical_rules: Normalize endpoints before admiring growth.
Prefer full-cycle comparisons over single-period rebounds.
example_buffett_phrasing: the base year was unusually weak or unusually strong

framework_items.section: Acquisitions should solve the scale problem, but only under strict conditions
framework_items.principle_statement: Acquisitions are useful only when they add meaningful earning power, are bought at a sensible price, and improve per-share intrinsic value.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Use acquisitions only when price, business quality, management quality, and financing all improve per-share intrinsic value.
metric_definition: earning_power: sustainable earnings contribution expected from the acquired business
purchase_price: economic consideration paid to obtain the business
per_share_intrinsic_value: intrinsic value attributable to each share after the transaction
metric_usage_context: earning_power: used to test whether the acquired business materially adds to owner economics
purchase_price: used to judge whether expected returns are sensible
per_share_intrinsic_value: used as the final test of whether the deal creates value for existing owners
metric_limitations: earning_power: optimistic projections can overstate deal quality
purchase_price: a fair business can become a poor investment at too high a price
per_share_intrinsic_value: total earnings growth can conceal per-share dilution
performance_logic: Acquisitions only help large capital bases when the business received has better economics than the value surrendered.
evaluation_questions: Does the company need acquisitions to sustain acceptable growth?
Is management disciplined on price?
Will the acquisition improve per-share intrinsic value?
Is the acquisition funded intelligently?
diagnostic_signals: Deal rhetoric focused on size not value
Use of undervalued stock as currency
Weak managers in the target business
practical_rules: Treat acquisitions as capital-allocation decisions, not growth theater.
Require sensible price and durable earning power.

framework_items.section: Stock-financed acquisitions are partly a sale of your existing business
framework_items.principle_title: Stock issuance has a real cost
framework_items.principle_statement: When management uses stock to buy another company, shareholders are also selling part of everything they already own.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Treat stock-financed acquisitions as an economic sale of existing ownership, not as costless currency.
metric_definition: shares_issued: new equity units created and transferred as acquisition consideration
dilution: reduction in each existing owner's claim on the business after new shares are issued
per_share_intrinsic_value: intrinsic value remaining for each share after issuance
metric_usage_context: shares_issued: used to measure the economic price paid with ownership rather than cash
dilution: used to assess the value surrendered by existing shareholders
per_share_intrinsic_value: used to determine whether the deal increases owner value after accounting for dilution
metric_limitations: shares_issued: management may treat stock as free when market prices are favorable
dilution: total earnings growth can hide a lower value per share
per_share_intrinsic_value: reported accretion may not equal real owner accretion
performance_logic: Issuing stock transfers a portion of existing business value to the seller; value is created only if the business received exceeds that surrendered.
evaluation_questions: What value is being given up by issuing shares?
Would the deal still make sense if paid in cash?
Is per-share intrinsic value higher after the deal?
diagnostic_signals: Management acts as if stock were monopoly money
Deal defended by total earnings accretion only
practical_rules: Count equity issuance as a real payment.
Judge stock deals on value received versus value surrendered.
example_buffett_phrasing: selling part of everything they already own
Issuing shares is an economic payment

framework_items.section: Buy businesses with excellent managers and let them run
framework_items.principle_statement: A wonderful business is strengthened when it is run by managers who think like owners, allocate rationally, and do not require bureaucracy from the parent.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Management quality is a major component of intrinsic value when managers are owner-oriented, operationally strong, and trustworthy with autonomy.
performance_logic: Strong managers widen the moat, control avoidable costs, and allocate capital rationally without requiring heavy centralized control.
evaluation_questions: Does management think like an owner?
Do they control unnecessary costs?
Can they be trusted with autonomy?
practical_rules: Treat management quality as part of business quality.
Prefer managers who improve the business without empire-building.

framework_items.section: A good acquisition target often has a strong culture, not just strong numbers
framework_items.principle_statement: Numbers matter, but operating attitude and cultural fit affect margins, capital allocation, durability, and integration risk.
framework_items.normalized_principle: A strong operating culture is an economic asset, not a decorative trait.
performance_logic: Disciplined culture supports efficiency, service quality, durable margins, and lower post-acquisition integration risk.
evaluation_questions: Does the company run a tight ship?
Does it avoid waste while still investing for advantage?
Will it remain strong without parent interference?
practical_rules: Include culture in acquisition analysis, not only reported numbers.
Look for disciplined operators who reinforce advantage.

framework_items.section: Insurance analysis: focus on float and its cost, not just premium volume
framework_items.principle_statement: For insurers, the key economic questions are how much float they hold and what that float costs over time.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Judge insurance operations by the amount and long-run cost of float rather than premium volume alone.
metric_definition: float: investable funds held from policyholder liabilities before claims are paid
underwriting_profit: profit or loss from insurance operations before investment income
combined_ratio: ratio summarizing underwriting profitability relative to premium earned
premium_volume: amount of insurance premiums written or earned
metric_usage_context: float: used to evaluate the scale of investable funds available to the insurer
underwriting_profit: used to determine whether float is better than free, free, or costly
combined_ratio: used to judge whether underwriting is disciplined over time
premium_volume: used only as a secondary indicator because growth alone does not show economic quality
metric_limitations: float: large float can be dangerous if generated at persistent underwriting losses
underwriting_profit: one year can be noisy and should be viewed over time
combined_ratio: a single-year ratio can mask cycle behavior
premium_volume: growth can hide value destruction when pricing is weak
performance_logic: Insurance creates value when it produces large investable float at zero or negative cost through disciplined underwriting.
evaluation_questions: How much float does the insurer hold?
What is the long-run cost of float?
Are combined ratios attractive over time?
Is management pursuing volume at the expense of standards?
practical_rules: Prioritize float quality over premium size.
Assess underwriting economics across the cycle.

framework_items.section: Underwriting discipline outranks growth in insurance
framework_items.principle_title: Shrink rather than write foolish business
framework_items.principle_statement: For insurers, underwriting discipline must trump all other goals, including volume growth.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Insurance growth is valuable only when pricing compensates for risk and management is willing to contract when pricing is inadequate.
metric_definition: combined_ratio: underwriting profitability indicator combining losses and expenses relative to premiums
premium_volume: amount of insurance business written
pricing_adequacy: whether premium pricing compensates for expected and uncertain losses
metric_usage_context: combined_ratio: used to verify whether discipline is preserved through the cycle
premium_volume: used as a warning sign when growth is pursued despite poor pricing
pricing_adequacy: used to decide whether to write, reduce, or refuse business
metric_limitations: combined_ratio: can look acceptable temporarily before long-tail losses emerge
premium_volume: higher volume can destroy value if pricing is inadequate
pricing_adequacy: difficult to assess under deep uncertainty
performance_logic: Writing less business at rational prices preserves long-term float quality and prevents value destruction from underpriced risk.
evaluation_questions: Does management refuse business when pricing is inadequate?
Has the company shown willingness to shrink in soft markets?
Are losses from bad luck or bad underwriting behavior?
practical_rules: Reward underwriting restraint, not premium growth.
Prefer insurers willing to contract when the market is irrational.
example_buffett_phrasing: Walk away when pricing is inadequate
shrink rather than write foolish business

framework_items.section: When uncertainty is high, demand a larger margin in price and smaller exposure
framework_items.principle_statement: When downside could be very large and probabilities are unclear, price more conservatively and cap exposure.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Under deep uncertainty, require more favorable pricing and limit position size or exposure.
metric_definition: expected_return: compensation expected for bearing the risk under uncertain outcomes
exposure_size: amount of capital or liability committed to a single uncertain risk
metric_usage_context: expected_return: used to demand a larger margin when model uncertainty is high
exposure_size: used to prevent one unknowable scenario from causing disproportionate damage
metric_limitations: expected_return: model-based estimates can understate tail risk
exposure_size: even a well-priced risk can become dangerous if sized too large
performance_logic: When probabilities are unclear, conservative pricing and smaller exposure reduce the chance that one bad scenario becomes distress.
evaluation_questions: What do we not know?
Could the downside be much worse than historical averages imply?
Are we being paid enough for model uncertainty?
If wrong, does the position cause distress or only pain?
practical_rules: Demand higher compensation where uncertainty is high.
Limit aggregate exposure to uncertain risks.
example_buffett_phrasing: demand a larger margin in price

framework_items.section: In long-tail risk businesses, reserve conservatively because surprises are asymmetric
framework_items.principle_statement: In long-tail liability businesses, unpleasant surprises are more common than pleasant ones.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Long-tail liabilities should be valued and reserved conservatively because adverse development is more likely than favorable surprise.
metric_definition: loss_reserves: liabilities recorded today for claims expected to be paid in the future
ultimate_losses: total eventual claims cost after all development is known
reserve_development: later change in previously recorded reserve estimates
metric_usage_context: loss_reserves: used to judge whether current earnings rely on optimistic assumptions
ultimate_losses: used to test whether original estimates were conservative
reserve_development: used to detect systematic optimism or prudence in management estimates
metric_limitations: loss_reserves: long-tail estimation is inherently uncertain and vulnerable to optimism
ultimate_losses: can take years to emerge, masking current-year weakness
reserve_development: favorable one-time development does not prove conservatism
performance_logic: Because liabilities settle far in the future, small estimation errors can compound into materially overstated earnings and understated risk.
evaluation_questions: Are liabilities short-tail or long-tail?
How much estimation is embedded in current earnings?
Are reserves conservative or optimistic?
What is management's history with adverse development?
diagnostic_signals: Current profits driven by reserve assumptions
practical_rules: Apply extra conservatism to long-tail liabilities.
Treat reserve optimism as a serious warning sign.

framework_items.section: Cost advantage can be converted into customer value, brand strength, and market share
framework_items.principle_title: Cost advantage widens the moat when shared
framework_items.principle_statement: A durable competitive advantage often starts with structural cost advantage that is partly passed on to customers.
framework_items.normalized_principle: A structural cost advantage becomes a stronger moat when management converts it into better customer value, retention, and share gains.
metric_definition: unit_cost: cost to serve or produce each unit relative to competitors
customer_price: price charged to the customer for the offering
retention_rate: share of customers who continue doing business with the company
market_share: proportion of total market demand captured by the company
conversion_rate: rate at which prospective customers become buyers
metric_usage_context: unit_cost: used to identify structural operating advantage
customer_price: used to see whether advantage is shared to reinforce value perception
retention_rate: used to confirm that customer value is strengthening the franchise
market_share: used to verify that cost advantage is translating into competitive gains
conversion_rate: used to test whether customer value is pulling in new business efficiently
metric_limitations: unit_cost: temporary cost advantages may not be durable
customer_price: lower pricing can destroy value if not backed by true cost advantage
retention_rate: can be influenced by switching frictions rather than customer delight
market_share: share gains can be bought uneconomically
conversion_rate: can be distorted by promotions or changing channel mix
performance_logic: Lower structural costs can be partly shared with customers, improving value perception, reinforcing the brand, and widening the moat profitably.
evaluation_questions: Does the company have a structural cost advantage?
Is the advantage being shared in a way that wins customers?
Are conversion, retention, and share trends confirming the moat?
practical_rules: Look for cost advantage that strengthens the customer proposition.
Separate profitable moat widening from uneconomic discounting.
example_buffett_phrasing: lower unit cost -> lower customer price / better value -> higher conversion and retention

framework_items.section: Brand strength matters, but proof comes from economics
framework_items.principle_title: Brand must show up in outcomes
framework_items.principle_statement: Brand should not be treated as a vague story; it should show up in conversion, retention, share gains, or pricing power.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Treat brand as credible only when economic evidence confirms it.
metric_definition: conversion_rate: share of prospects converted into customers
retention_rate: share of customers retained over time
market_share: proportion of market demand captured
pricing_power: ability to raise or sustain price without losing economic strength
metric_usage_context: conversion_rate: used as evidence that the brand pulls customers efficiently
retention_rate: used to show durable customer attachment
market_share: used to confirm that the brand supports competitive gains
pricing_power: used to test whether the brand supports value capture or value leadership
metric_limitations: conversion_rate: can be lifted temporarily by promotion
retention_rate: can overstate true loyalty when switching costs are high
market_share: can rise through uneconomic pricing
pricing_power: apparent pricing power can fade when competition responds
performance_logic: A brand is economically meaningful only if it improves customer behavior or pricing outcomes in a durable way.
evaluation_questions: Is the brand pulling customers more efficiently?
Does it improve retention?
Does it support premium pricing or superior volume?
diagnostic_signals: Evidence that marketing spend strengthens economics
practical_rules: Require operating evidence before crediting a brand moat.
Tie brand claims to measurable outcomes.
example_buffett_phrasing: Brand should not be treated as a vague story

framework_items.section: Regulated businesses can be excellent capital deployment vehicles even without outsized returns
framework_items.principle_title: Fair returns can still be attractive at scale
framework_items.principle_statement: A business does not need exceptional margins to be attractive if it can absorb large amounts of capital at fair, dependable returns.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Fair-return businesses can be excellent when they can safely absorb large amounts of capital and compound predictably.
metric_definition: return_on_capital: return earned on capital invested in the business
reinvestment_capacity: amount of capital the business can deploy at acceptable economics
earnings_stability: dependability of recurring earnings streams
metric_usage_context: return_on_capital: used to judge whether the business earns fair returns rather than exceptional ones
reinvestment_capacity: used to determine whether large amounts of capital can be put to work safely
earnings_stability: used to support the case for financing and long-term compounding
metric_limitations: return_on_capital: a lower but dependable return can be superior to a higher but tiny opportunity set
reinvestment_capacity: scale can tempt overinvestment if returns fall below fair levels
earnings_stability: regulated stability can still be exposed to policy or cost shocks
performance_logic: A large capital base needs places to deploy money safely; fair, durable returns on big asset bases can be highly valuable.
evaluation_questions: Can the business deploy large amounts of capital?
Are returns fair and dependable?
How stable are the earnings streams?
diagnostic_signals: Ability to finance recurring asset growth
practical_rules: Value scale and dependability alongside headline margins.
Match capital-hungry owners with businesses that can absorb capital safely.
example_buffett_phrasing: absorb large amounts of capital at fair, dependable returns

framework_items.section: Debt is safer when backed by diverse, stable, uncorrelated earnings streams
framework_items.principle_statement: Risk depends not only on leverage ratios but also on the quality and diversification of the earnings supporting debt.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Debt safety depends on the resilience, diversity, and correlation profile of the cash flows behind it.
metric_definition: interest_coverage: extent to which operating earnings can cover interest obligations
debt_level: amount of leverage carried by the enterprise
earnings_diversification: breadth and independence of earnings sources across operations or geographies
cash_flow_stability: durability of cash generation under stress
metric_usage_context: interest_coverage: used together with earnings quality rather than in isolation
debt_level: used to judge whether obligations are sensible relative to resilient cash flows
earnings_diversification: used to assess whether one shock can threaten the whole enterprise
cash_flow_stability: used to test survival under adverse scenarios
metric_limitations: interest_coverage: a high ratio can still be fragile if tied to one cyclical asset
debt_level: cosmetic leverage ratios can understate structural fragility
earnings_diversification: correlations can rise in stress
cash_flow_stability: past stability may not persist under regime change
performance_logic: Stable, diversified, and weakly correlated earnings streams make a given level of debt safer than concentrated or fragile cash flows would.
evaluation_questions: What earnings stream services the debt?
How stable is it?
How diversified is it?
Would one shock threaten the whole enterprise?
diagnostic_signals: Coverage that remains resilient in stress
practical_rules: Analyze debt against the cash flows behind it, not ratios alone.
Prefer balance sheets built for survival.

framework_items.section: Prefer economic reality to accounting cosmetics
framework_items.principle_statement: Do not celebrate accounting improvements that do not improve owner economics.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Base judgments on owner economics rather than accounting presentation.
metric_definition: reported_earnings: accounting earnings shown under reported financial statements
owner_earnings: economic earnings available to owners after necessary business requirements
leverage: economic obligations that may be hidden or flattered by presentation choices
metric_usage_context: reported_earnings: used only after testing whether they reflect real economics
owner_earnings: used as the more relevant measure of true business benefit to owners
leverage: used to see whether financial risk is being obscured by structure or presentation
metric_limitations: reported_earnings: can be distorted by accounting conventions and transactions designed to flatter appearance
owner_earnings: requires judgment and may not map cleanly to GAAP figures
leverage: apparent improvement can come from cosmetic restructuring rather than lower economic risk
performance_logic: Accounting can change appearance without changing economics; value depends on what owners truly gain, not on prettier statements.
evaluation_questions: Does this accounting result reflect real economic gain?
Is management optimizing optics or economics?
What would owner earnings look like without presentation distortions?
practical_rules: Adjust reported numbers to economic reality.
Distrust actions taken mainly to improve appearance.
example_buffett_phrasing: Prefer economic reality to accounting cosmetics

framework_items.section: Ignore accounting gains that do not change economic ownership or economic value
framework_items.principle_statement: Not all GAAP earnings are economically meaningful, especially when no real sale, tax outflow, or ownership change has occurred.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Exclude accounting gains from normalized earnings when they do not change economic ownership or intrinsic value.
metric_definition: reported_gain: accounting profit recognized under reporting rules
normalized_earnings: earnings adjusted to reflect recurring economic reality
tax_outflow: actual tax payment triggered by an economically meaningful gain
metric_usage_context: reported_gain: used as a candidate item for removal when it is non-economic
normalized_earnings: used to estimate recurring owner economics
tax_outflow: used to test whether the gain had real economic consequences
metric_limitations: reported_gain: can flatter reported results without changing intrinsic value
normalized_earnings: adjustments can be subjective if overused
tax_outflow: absence of tax does not always prove no economic gain, but it is a useful clue
performance_logic: A reported gain that does not change ownership economics, cash consequences, or business value should not be treated as recurring earning power.
evaluation_questions: Is this gain economically real?
Did ownership economics change?
Did taxes actually become payable?
Should the item be excluded from normalized earnings?
diagnostic_signals: No meaningful change in ownership economics
practical_rules: Strip non-economic gains out of valuation work.
Use normalized earnings for intrinsic value estimation.
example_buffett_phrasing: Ignore accounting gains that do not change economic ownership or economic value

framework_items.section: The quality of a CEO can massively change intrinsic value
framework_items.principle_statement: A great CEO can impose discipline, improve operations, strengthen marketing, and raise intrinsic value sharply.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Leadership quality can materially raise or reduce intrinsic value even when the franchise is unchanged.
metric_definition: return_on_capital: returns generated on capital under management's stewardship
margin: profitability achieved through operations and pricing discipline
market_position: strength of the company's competitive standing under leadership
metric_usage_context: return_on_capital: used to judge capital allocation under a CEO's tenure
margin: used to test operational improvement or decline
market_position: used to assess whether leadership is strengthening the franchise
metric_limitations: return_on_capital: can move with industry conditions, not only leadership quality
margin: temporary margin expansion may come from short-term cuts that harm the moat
market_position: strategic gains can take time to verify
performance_logic: Leadership affects economics through capital allocation, operating discipline, marketing execution, and cultural standards.
evaluation_questions: Has the CEO improved capital allocation?
Have margins or market position improved under their tenure?
Is the CEO creating value or just reporting activity?
practical_rules: Treat CEO assessment as part of intrinsic value analysis.
Judge leaders by long-term economics, not charisma.
example_buffett_phrasing: The quality of a CEO can massively change intrinsic value

framework_items.section: Capital allocation is judged by value received versus value given up
framework_items.principle_statement: The deepest yardstick is what the company received in business value versus what it gave up.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Judge every capital-allocation decision by whether the value received exceeds the value surrendered on a per-share basis.
metric_definition: intrinsic_value_received: business value or earning power obtained from a decision
intrinsic_value_given_up: cash, stock, debt capacity, or alternative opportunity surrendered
per_share_intrinsic_value: value remaining for each share after the decision
metric_usage_context: intrinsic_value_received: used to assess acquisitions, projects, or repurchases
intrinsic_value_given_up: used to quantify the true cost of a capital-allocation move
per_share_intrinsic_value: used as the final test of whether owners benefit
metric_limitations: intrinsic_value_received: optimistic assumptions can overstate benefits
intrinsic_value_given_up: hidden dilution or leverage can understate cost
per_share_intrinsic_value: total company growth can conceal worse economics for each share
performance_logic: Capital allocation creates value only when what owners receive economically is worth more than what they surrender.
evaluation_questions: What exactly was surrendered?
What exactly was received?
Was intrinsic value per share increased?
Would the decision still look attractive if owners saw the payment in cash?
diagnostic_signals: Focus on scale instead of value exchange
practical_rules: Apply the same value exchange test to acquisitions, buybacks, debt issuance, and internal projects.
example_buffett_phrasing: value received versus value given up

framework_items.section: Retained earnings are not automatically value-creating
framework_items.principle_title: Retain only if returns justify it
framework_items.principle_statement: Keeping earnings inside the company is beneficial only if management can deploy them at attractive returns.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Retained earnings are valuable only when management can reinvest incremental capital at attractive returns.
metric_definition: retained_earnings: earnings kept inside the company rather than distributed to owners
incremental_return_on_capital: return earned on additional capital retained and reinvested
dividend_payout: cash distributed to shareholders instead of being retained
metric_usage_context: retained_earnings: used to test whether managers are deploying owner capital productively
incremental_return_on_capital: used as the key benchmark for whether retention creates value
dividend_payout: used as the alternative when internal opportunities are weak
metric_limitations: retained_earnings: retention can create size without value
incremental_return_on_capital: accounting returns can overstate true economic returns
dividend_payout: distributing cash can be superior even if it lowers reported growth
performance_logic: Nondistributed earnings help owners only when the company can reinvest them at returns superior to the owners' alternatives.
evaluation_questions: What return is management earning on retained capital?
Would owners be better off receiving dividends?
Is management retaining cash because it has opportunities or because it prefers empire?
diagnostic_signals: Rising retained capital with mediocre returns
practical_rules: Measure retained earnings by incremental returns, not by size growth.
Favor payout when reinvestment is unattractive.
example_buffett_phrasing: Retained earnings are not automatically value-creating

framework_items.section: Compensation design can sever the alignment between managers and owners
framework_items.principle_statement: Badly designed options and compensation plans can reward managers even when owners do not truly prosper.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Compensation design should reward real value creation rather than mechanical EPS effects or passive benefits from retained capital.
metric_definition: stock_options: manager compensation tied to a fixed strike price on company shares
earnings_per_share: reported earnings attributable to each share
retained_earnings: capital kept inside the company that can mechanically lift option value
metric_usage_context: stock_options: used to assess whether managers share upside without equal downside
earnings_per_share: used to test whether pay design rewards mechanical per-share effects rather than real value creation
retained_earnings: used to examine whether managers benefit from undistributed owner capital without proper adjustment
metric_limitations: stock_options: fixed-price options can reward managers from time and retained capital even without improved economics
earnings_per_share: EPS can rise from buybacks or leverage without value creation
retained_earnings: retained capital may increase option value even if owners would have preferred dividends
performance_logic: Compensation design shapes behavior; poorly aligned incentives can quietly transfer value from owners to managers.
evaluation_questions: Does management get rewarded for true value creation or for mechanical EPS effects?
Are option strike prices adjusted for retained earnings?
Does the plan encourage poor capital allocation?
diagnostic_signals: Perks and severance that reward failure
Options benefiting from passive capital retention
practical_rules: Examine pay structure, not just pay magnitude.
Prefer incentives tied to genuine owner value creation.
example_buffett_phrasing: Compensation design can sever the alignment between managers and owners

framework_items.section: Moat widening happens through many small daily actions
framework_items.principle_statement: Competitive position gets stronger or weaker every day through customer service, cost control, product improvement, and operating habits.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Moats widen or shrink through cumulative daily operating behavior, not through slogans alone.
metric_definition: customer_retention: durability of customer relationships over time
cost_position: relative cost advantage versus competitors
product_quality: strength of the customer offering in use and perception
market_share: competitive position captured over time
metric_usage_context: customer_retention: used to detect whether customers are being delighted or neglected
cost_position: used to see whether operational discipline is improving or eroding
product_quality: used to judge whether reinvestment is reinforcing the franchise
market_share: used as an outcome signal of moat strengthening or weakening
metric_limitations: customer_retention: can lag underlying deterioration
cost_position: short-term cuts can improve costs while harming the franchise
product_quality: hard to quantify cleanly in early stages
market_share: share can hold temporarily before the moat weakens visibly
performance_logic: Daily operating choices accumulate into stronger customer loyalty, better economics, and wider competitive advantage.
evaluation_questions: Are customer experience, cost position, and product quality improving?
Are operating habits compounding positively or negatively?
What evidence shows the franchise is stronger than 3-5 years ago?
practical_rules: Evaluate the moat through operating evidence, not only narrative.
Look for compounding daily habits that reinforce advantage.
example_buffett_phrasing: Moat widening happens through many small daily actions

framework_items.section: When short-term earnings conflict with moat widening, choose the moat
framework_items.principle_statement: Short-term profit optimization can destroy long-term franchise value if management underinvests in service, brand, or operations.
framework_items.normalized_principle: When current earnings and long-term competitive strength conflict, favor decisions that preserve or widen the moat.
metric_definition: short_term_earnings: current-period reported profits
brand_strength: economic strength of the brand reflected in customer behavior
customer_retention: ongoing customer loyalty over time
metric_usage_context: short_term_earnings: used to detect whether near-term targets are driving harmful decisions
brand_strength: used to judge whether the franchise is being protected or weakened
customer_retention: used to test whether underinvestment is damaging customer relationships
metric_limitations: short_term_earnings: current profit can be overstated by cutting investment that supports future economics
brand_strength: damage may emerge with a lag
customer_retention: deterioration may appear after a delay
performance_logic: Sacrificing essential reinvestment can boost current profit while quietly weakening future economics and intrinsic value.
evaluation_questions: Is management underinvesting to hit current targets?
Are cost cuts intelligent or franchise-damaging?
Is long-term competitive position improving even if current profit is lower?
diagnostic_signals: Deferred maintenance of brand or service
Temporary margin lift with weaker franchise indicators
practical_rules: Prefer intelligent reinvestment over short-term cosmetic profit.
Judge earnings quality against franchise health.
example_buffett_phrasing: When short-term earnings conflict with moat widening, choose the moat

framework_items.section: Passionate, high-agency managers are a strategic asset
framework_items.principle_statement: The best managers act like founders or owner-operators, widen the moat without being told, and do not need constant oversight.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Passionate, high-agency managers are strategic assets because they improve the business without bureaucratic prompting.
performance_logic: Engaged operators spot opportunities, solve problems early, and reinforce competitive strength more effectively than passive caretakers.
evaluation_questions: Is the manager energized by the business itself?
Do results suggest high agency and operating passion?
Would the business remain excellent under decentralized ownership?
practical_rules: Favor managers who act like owners and builders, not administrators.

framework_items.section: Derivatives and opaque financial contracts create hidden, nonlinear risk
framework_items.principle_statement: Complex financial contracts with long durations, model-based values, and hard-to-observe exits can be dangerous even when they appear manageable.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Avoid or heavily discount businesses whose economics depend on opaque, model-driven, long-dated financial contracts.
metric_definition: mark_to_model_value: estimated contract value based on internal assumptions rather than observable prices
duration: length of time over which contractual exposures remain outstanding
liquidity: ability to exit or offset positions at reasonable cost
counterparty_risk: risk that the other side cannot perform when stress arrives
metric_usage_context: mark_to_model_value: used to identify reliance on assumptions rather than realized economics
duration: used to assess how long uncertainty and exposure persist
liquidity: used to judge whether positions can be exited in bad markets
counterparty_risk: used to assess nonlinear stress outcomes
metric_limitations: mark_to_model_value: reported profits can overstate economic reality
duration: long-dated contracts increase uncertainty and path dependence
liquidity: apparent liquidity can vanish during stress
counterparty_risk: can become highly correlated in crises
performance_logic: Opaque contracts embed hidden tail risks, especially when profits are recognized before cash reality is known and exits depend on calm markets.
evaluation_questions: How observable is the true value of the position?
Could liquidation become impossible under stress?
Are profits dependent on internal models rather than realized economics?
Does compensation encourage risk understatement?
practical_rules: Apply deep skepticism to opaque derivative exposure.
Favor understandable contracts with observable economics.

framework_items.section: When a serious problem is identified, act immediately
framework_items.principle_statement: A bad business, a bad risk book, or a bad personnel situation often becomes worse when management hesitates.
framework_items.normalized_principle: When a serious economic problem is identified, decisive action usually reduces total damage.
performance_logic: Problems in weak businesses or risk books often compound over time; delay raises eventual economic cost.
evaluation_questions: Is management confronting the problem directly?
Has delay increased economic damage?
Would an immediate painful fix be cheaper than gradual drift?
practical_rules: Move quickly when the economics of a mistake are clear.
Do not let loyalty or hope replace action.

framework_items.section: For owners in aggregate, investment returns come from business returns minus friction
framework_items.principle_title: Owner returns are reduced by friction
framework_items.principle_statement: Investors as a group cannot extract more than businesses as a group earn, except by redistributing among themselves; after costs, they earn less.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Aggregate owner returns equal business returns minus frictional costs.
metric_definition: business_return: underlying economic return generated by the owned businesses
fees: explicit charges paid to intermediaries or managers
turnover_cost: costs created by trading activity and repositioning
incentive_allocation: share of profits diverted to agents or managers
metric_usage_context: business_return: used as the starting point for what owners in aggregate can receive
fees: used to quantify how much gross business return is siphoned away
turnover_cost: used to measure the drag from activity
incentive_allocation: used to assess whether helpers capture too much of the upside
metric_limitations: business_return: can be obscured by temporary market moves
fees: small annual fees compound into large long-term drags
turnover_cost: often hidden in execution spreads and taxes
incentive_allocation: asymmetric incentive structures can look harmless before compounding
performance_logic: Owners as a group cannot outperform the businesses they own once intermediation costs are deducted; friction reduces net compounding.
evaluation_questions: What are the underlying economics of the business or basket?
How much friction stands between business return and owner return?
Is the strategy adding enough value to overcome its costs?
practical_rules: Start with business economics, then subtract all friction.
Be skeptical of structures that promise aggregate outperformance after costs.
example_buffett_phrasing: returns come from business returns minus friction

framework_items.section: For investors as a whole, motion usually reduces return
framework_items.principle_title: Activity is often a tax on returns
framework_items.principle_statement: Excessive trading, manager layering, and expensive intermediation reduce net owner results.
framework_items.normalized_principle: For investors in aggregate, more motion usually means lower net returns.
metric_definition: portfolio_turnover: frequency with which holdings are bought and sold
transaction_cost: explicit and implicit costs created by trading activity
net_return: return remaining for owners after costs
metric_usage_context: portfolio_turnover: used to diagnose unnecessary activity
transaction_cost: used to quantify the drag created by motion
net_return: used to judge whether activity is creating or destroying owner value
metric_limitations: portfolio_turnover: some activity is necessary and not all turnover is wasteful
transaction_cost: many costs are hidden and underestimated
net_return: short-term gains from activity may not persist after costs
performance_logic: Trading and intermediation consume part of business returns, so motion usually lowers what owners keep.
evaluation_questions: Is activity creating value or just cost?
What turnover is truly necessary?
Would owners do better by sitting still?
practical_rules: Prefer patience unless activity has a clear economic edge.
Treat motion as a cost that needs justification.
example_buffett_phrasing: More motion usually means less net return

framework_items.section: Favor structures where the owner captures most of the economics
framework_items.principle_statement: High-fee or asymmetric structures can siphon away a meaningful share of business earnings.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Prefer ownership structures in which the principal keeps most of the economics and incentives are symmetrical.
metric_definition: fixed_fee: recurring charge taken regardless of investment outcome
performance_fee: share of gains allocated to managers or intermediaries
owner_share_of_return: portion of gross business return ultimately retained by the true owner
metric_usage_context: fixed_fee: used to assess baseline drag even when no value is added
performance_fee: used to judge whether upside is shared asymmetrically
owner_share_of_return: used as the final measure of whether the structure serves owners well
metric_limitations: fixed_fee: compounding drag can be severe over long periods
performance_fee: asymmetry can encourage risk-taking not borne equally by managers
owner_share_of_return: gross returns can look attractive while owner capture is poor
performance_logic: When helpers take too much of the upside and little of the downside, owners lose a large share of the economics.
evaluation_questions: Who gets the economics?
Are incentives symmetric?
How much of the gross return reaches the true owner?
diagnostic_signals: Structures enriching helpers more than principals
practical_rules: Prefer low-friction, owner-friendly structures.
Discount high-fee arrangements heavily.
example_buffett_phrasing: Favor structures where the owner captures most of the economics

framework_items.section: Favor equity exposure with positive economics over hedges with negative carry, when appropriate
framework_items.principle_title: Implement macro views through productive assets when possible
framework_items.principle_statement: When seeking exposure to a macro theme, compare direct instruments with ownership of productive businesses and consider carry costs.
framework_items.normalized_principle: When expressing a macro view, prefer vehicles with productive underlying economics over negative-carry instruments when they fit the thesis.
metric_definition: carry_cost: economic drag from holding a position over time
equity_return: return generated by owning productive businesses
currency_exposure: sensitivity of value to foreign-currency movements
metric_usage_context: carry_cost: used to judge whether a direct hedge leaks value while waiting for the thesis to play out
equity_return: used to compare a productive asset alternative with a pure hedge
currency_exposure: used to match the implementation vehicle to the macro thesis
metric_limitations: carry_cost: negative carry can erode gains even if the macro idea is right
equity_return: productive assets add business risk beyond the macro exposure
currency_exposure: indirect exposure may not match a direct hedge perfectly
performance_logic: A thesis can be correct but poorly implemented; productive assets may provide better economics than pure hedges if they express the same view.
evaluation_questions: Is there a better vehicle for the same macro exposure?
What is the carry cost?
Does the chosen instrument have productive underlying economics?
practical_rules: Separate thesis quality from implementation quality.
Prefer productive exposure when it expresses the same view acceptably.
example_buffett_phrasing: positive economics over hedges with negative carry

framework_items.section: A business can be excellent even in a weak industry if it has operational superiority and financing advantage
framework_items.principle_title: Relative business quality still matters in bad industries
framework_items.principle_statement: An unattractive industry does not automatically imply an unattractive company.
framework_items.normalized_principle: A company can outperform a weak industry when it has structural operating or financing advantages relative to peers.
metric_definition: cost_advantage: relative efficiency advantage versus competitors
funding_cost: cost and availability of financing relative to peers
market_share: portion of industry demand won by the company
metric_usage_context: cost_advantage: used to identify operational superiority in a troubled industry
funding_cost: used to assess whether capital access creates a competitive edge
market_share: used to confirm that the advantaged firm is taking share from weaker rivals
metric_limitations: cost_advantage: industry decline can overwhelm even strong operators
funding_cost: financing edge can narrow if capital markets normalize
market_share: share gains do not guarantee attractive industry economics overall
performance_logic: When weaker competitors retreat, firms with better operations and funding can consolidate demand and earn strong relative results even in a poor industry.
evaluation_questions: Is the company structurally advantaged versus peers?
Does funding advantage create opportunity?
Is the business winning while the industry struggles?
practical_rules: Look for relative superiority, not just industry averages.
Distinguish bad industries from bad businesses.
example_buffett_phrasing: excellent even in a weak industry

framework_items.section: Look at returns on tangible capital, but also remember purchase premium matters
framework_items.principle_title: Business quality and purchase price are separate
framework_items.principle_statement: A business may earn high returns on tangible net worth yet deliver lower returns on the investor's carrying value if the purchase price included substantial goodwill.
framework_items.normalized_principle: High returns on tangible capital do not guarantee high returns on the owner's total purchase price.
metric_definition: return_on_tangible_capital: return generated on the tangible capital actually used in operations
goodwill: purchase premium paid above tangible net assets in an acquisition or valuation
return_on_purchase_price: return earned on the total capital the owner actually paid
metric_usage_context: return_on_tangible_capital: used to judge business quality and operating efficiency
goodwill: used to identify the gap between operating economics and investor carrying value
return_on_purchase_price: used to judge investment attractiveness after price paid
metric_limitations: return_on_tangible_capital: can overstate the investor's return when purchase premium is large
goodwill: accounting goodwill is not itself productive capital
return_on_purchase_price: may look modest even for a wonderful business if bought dearly
performance_logic: A great business can be a merely fair investment when the owner pays a large premium above the capital actually employed in the business.
evaluation_questions: What are returns on tangible operating capital?
What premium was paid?
What return does the business earn on the actual price the owner paid?
diagnostic_signals: High operating returns with heavy goodwill
Strong business quality but only fair investment economics
practical_rules: Separate business quality from purchase price discipline.
Evaluate both operating returns and return on invested purchase price.

framework_items.section: Raw-material or energy pressure tests franchise strength
framework_items.principle_statement: A good business can absorb cost inflation better than a weak one through pricing power, efficiency, brand, or execution.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Input-cost shocks are a useful test of pricing power and franchise resilience.
metric_definition: input_cost_inflation: rise in raw-material or energy costs that pressure economics
gross_margin: profit retained after direct costs
pricing_power: ability to pass higher costs through without damaging demand materially
metric_usage_context: input_cost_inflation: used as a stress test of business resilience
gross_margin: used to see how much cost pressure the business can absorb
pricing_power: used to judge whether the franchise can reprice successfully
metric_limitations: input_cost_inflation: short-term lags can temporarily understate pricing power
gross_margin: margin changes can reflect mix as well as pricing power
pricing_power: apparent pass-through ability may fade if competition intensifies
performance_logic: Businesses with stronger franchises absorb or pass through inflation better, preserving economics when weak competitors struggle.
evaluation_questions: Can the company pass through input inflation?
How long is the lag?
Are margins resilient through cost shocks?
practical_rules: Use inflation periods to test franchise quality.
Study both pricing lag and margin resilience.
example_buffett_phrasing: Raw-material or energy pressure tests franchise strength

framework_items.section: Same-store sales, market share, conversion, and retention are moat indicators
framework_items.principle_title: Simple operating metrics can reveal moat strength early
framework_items.principle_statement: Operating indicators often reveal franchise strength earlier than accounting ratios do.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Track unit-level operating metrics to assess moat strength before it is fully visible in accounting results.
metric_definition: same_store_sales: sales growth from existing locations or units
market_share: portion of market demand captured
conversion_rate: rate at which prospects become customers
retention_rate: share of customers who stay over time
productivity: output or economics generated per unit of resource
metric_usage_context: same_store_sales: used to test whether existing units remain healthy
market_share: used to confirm franchise gains or erosion
conversion_rate: used to detect customer pull and value proposition strength
retention_rate: used to assess loyalty and satisfaction
productivity: used to see whether operational improvements are structural
metric_limitations: same_store_sales: can be distorted by pricing or unusual comparisons
market_share: gains can be bought uneconomically
conversion_rate: promotions can temporarily boost results
retention_rate: high retention may partly reflect switching friction
productivity: temporary efficiency measures may not persist
performance_logic: Operational metrics often capture customer preference and competitive strength before reported accounting ratios fully reflect them.
evaluation_questions: Are unit-level metrics strengthening?
Are customers choosing the business more often and staying longer?
Are productivity gains structural or temporary?
practical_rules: Use operating indicators as leading signals of moat health.
Validate accounting results with unit-level evidence.
example_buffett_phrasing: same-store sales, market share, conversion, and retention are moat indicators

framework_items.section: Buy to keep, not to flip
framework_items.principle_statement: The purchase decision should stand on the assumption of long-term ownership rather than near-term resale.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Evaluate businesses as if they will be owned for a decade or longer, not flipped on a market rerating.
performance_logic: A long-duration ownership lens forces better discipline on business quality, management quality, debt, durability, and purchase price.
evaluation_questions: Would this still be attractive if owned for a decade or longer?
Is the thesis dependent on rerating or on business compounding?
Would we be comfortable owning it through adversity?
diagnostic_signals: Reliance on multiple expansion rather than business progress
practical_rules: Underwrite on business compounding, not resale assumptions.
Prefer businesses you would be content to hold through adversity.
example_buffett_phrasing: Buy to keep, not to flip

framework_items.section: Avoid leverage that can produce ruin, even if it may raise average returns
framework_items.principle_statement: Buffett is willing to give up some upside to eliminate the possibility of permanent failure.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Avoid leverage structures that can cause ruin even if they improve average expected returns.
metric_definition: debt_maturity: timing profile of obligations that must be repaid or rolled
liquidity: resources available to meet obligations under stress
solvency: ability of the enterprise to remain financially viable after adverse events
refinancing_risk: dependence on market access to replace maturing obligations
metric_usage_context: debt_maturity: used to assess whether one stress period can force failure
liquidity: used to determine whether the company can absorb shocks without distress
solvency: used as the true risk yardstick rather than average-return enhancement
refinancing_risk: used to test whether the business survives if markets close
metric_limitations: debt_maturity: seemingly manageable ladders can become dangerous when earnings fall
liquidity: apparent liquidity may vanish if assets are illiquid under stress
solvency: average outcomes can hide catastrophic tail scenarios
refinancing_risk: reliance on friendly markets is a structural weakness
performance_logic: Leverage that raises expected return but introduces a credible path to failure destroys the compounding machine.
evaluation_questions: Could one bad scenario cause forced dilution or failure?
Does the company rely on refinancing confidence?
What happens if markets close?
practical_rules: Design balance sheets to survive extreme stress.
Accept lower upside in exchange for eliminating ruin risk.
example_buffett_phrasing: Avoid leverage that can produce ruin

framework_items.section: Risk is not volatility; risk is the chance of financial distress or permanent impairment
framework_items.principle_title: Define risk by impairment, not price movement
framework_items.principle_statement: A sound enterprise can tolerate volatility if it has ample net worth, strong liquidity, durable earnings streams, and manageable obligations.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Risk is the possibility of distress or permanent capital impairment, not mere mark-to-market volatility.
metric_definition: volatility: fluctuation in market prices or reported values over time
liquidity: cash resources available to meet obligations and seize time to recover
net_worth: capital cushion protecting the enterprise from shocks
fixed_obligations: contractual commitments that must be met regardless of business conditions
metric_usage_context: volatility: used as a secondary concern unless it creates forced action
liquidity: used to judge whether the firm can survive temporary shocks
net_worth: used to assess how much adversity can be absorbed
fixed_obligations: used to determine whether a temporary shock can become a permanent impairment
metric_limitations: volatility: high price movement alone does not equal economic danger
liquidity: strong liquidity can be temporary if based on unstable markets
net_worth: accounting net worth may overstate true protection if assets are overstated
fixed_obligations: hidden obligations can make the risk larger than it appears
performance_logic: Temporary price swings are tolerable when the enterprise has the balance-sheet strength and earnings durability to avoid forced action.
evaluation_questions: What could cause permanent capital loss here?
How strong is liquidity under stress?
Are obligations fixed while asset values are volatile?
Can the business survive extreme but plausible adversity?
diagnostic_signals: No forced selling or refinancing need
practical_rules: Focus first on permanent impairment risk.
Treat volatility as dangerous mainly when it interacts with leverage or forced action.
example_buffett_phrasing: chance of financial distress or permanent impairment

framework_items.section: Never forget the zero-risk of ruin: many successes multiplied by one zero still equal zero
framework_items.principle_title: One terminal loss wipes out compounding
framework_items.principle_statement: A long record of strong performance is meaningless if one event wipes out the enterprise.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Protect the compounding machine from any single event that can drive value to zero or near-zero.
performance_logic: Compounding depends on survival; one terminal failure destroys the value of many prior successes.
evaluation_questions: What single point of failure exists?
Could one extreme event reduce the company to zero or near-zero?
Are management and owners behaving as if survival comes first?
diagnostic_signals: Concentration that can erase decades of gains
practical_rules: Eliminate structures with credible paths to ruin.
Stress test for one-event failure, not only average outcomes.
example_buffett_phrasing: many successes multiplied by one zero still equal zero

framework_items.section: Board alignment matters because governance quality matters when hard choices arise
framework_items.principle_title: Boards matter most in hard moments
framework_items.principle_statement: Governance quality shows up when compensation must be disciplined, succession handled, or underperformance confronted.
framework_items.normalized_principle: Board alignment and independence matter most when difficult owner-protecting decisions must be made.
performance_logic: Aligned and independent boards protect owners when leadership changes, pay design, or underperformance create uncomfortable but necessary decisions.
evaluation_questions: Are directors economically aligned with owners?
Are they independent enough to make difficult decisions?
Would they protect shareholder value over personal comfort?
diagnostic_signals: Ability to act on succession and pay
practical_rules: Evaluate governance by its behavior in hard choices, not ceremonial compliance.
